title: Fermented rice water hair rinse
subtitle: A fermented rice water rinse used after shampooing to add strength and shine to the hair
excerpt: Fermenting rice water for 24 to 48 hours produces a mild lactic acid solution used in Asian hair care traditions. Applied after shampooing and rinsed out, it adds a coating to the hair shaft. This is a no-preservative fresh preparation; make a small amount weekly and store in the refrigerator.
sourceNotes: Fermented rice water in hair care: traditional practice among Yao women in Huangluo, China, documented in Journal of Cosmetic Science (2010), Ko et al. Inositol in rice water and its effect on hair: Robbins, Chemical and Physical Behaviour of Human Hair (2012), 5th ed. pH of fermented rice water: approximately 3.5 to 4.5 after 24 to 48 hours at room temperature.

recipe.yieldDescription: 250 ml rinse (1 application or 2 to 3 diluted uses)
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ferment the rice water for 24 to 48 hours at room temperature. Refrigerate after straining and use within 7 days.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Use within 7 days of straining. Keep refrigerated. Discard if the rinse develops an unpleasant or strongly sour smell beyond the usual mild sourness, or becomes cloudy or visibly active. This preparation contains no preservative.

glossaryTerms.definition: An organic acid produced during fermentation. In hair care, a mild lactic acid rinse smooths the hair cuticle and adjusts the pH of the hair shaft.

content.text: Rice water has been used as a hair rinse for centuries in parts of Asia. When the rinse water from washed rice is left to ferment at room temperature for 24 to 48 hours, beneficial yeasts and bacteria convert sugars into

. The pH of the fermented liquid falls to around 4 to 4.5, which is slightly below the natural pH of hair. Applied as a rinse after shampooing, it helps smooth the hair cuticle and adds a coating to the shaft. Inositol, a carbohydrate present in rice, is water-soluble and may penetrate damaged hair fibres.

items.prepNote: room temperature tap water is fine

content.text: Rinse the rice briefly under cold water to remove surface dust. Combine the rinsed rice and 400 ml of water in a clean jar. Stir briefly and let stand at room temperature for 30 minutes, then strain the rice water into a clean jar. Discard the rice (or cook it).

Cover the jar of rice water loosely with a cloth or lid that allows air flow. Leave at room temperature for 24 to 48 hours. The liquid will develop a mild, slightly sour smell as fermentation proceeds; this is normal. Do not leave longer than 48 hours at room temperature or the liquid becomes too acidic. Strain again through a fine sieve before use.

Dilute 2 to 3 tablespoons (30 to 45 ml) of fermented rice water into 200 ml of water before applying. Using it undiluted can be drying on fine or low-porosity hair. Start with a higher dilution ratio and adjust based on how the hair responds.

Shampoo and rinse as normal. Pour or squeeze the diluted rice water rinse over the hair. Leave for 1 to 5 minutes, then rinse thoroughly with water. Condition if the hair feels stiff afterwards; the rice water rinse alone is drying on very dry hair types.

Refrigerate after straining and use within 7 days. The liquid has no preservative and will continue to ferment in the refrigerator, becoming progressively more acidic. Discard if it smells strongly unpleasant, becomes thick, or shows visible growth.
